risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A monitoring agency prepared the randomization list and provided sealed envelopes for treatment allocation." Comment: The paper explicitly reports that a randomization list was prepared, and sealed envelopes were used for allocation. Although the exact random method (e.g., computer-generated random numbers) is not stated, the description is consistent with a randomized allocation sequence and does not indicate any predictable or non-random rule. Given the report of a randomized 1:1 assignment and preparation of a randomization list by an independent monitoring agency, this is judged low risk for sequence generation.

Quote: "A monitoring agency prepared the randomization list and provided sealed envelopes for treatment allocation." Comment: This explicitly states a concealment mechanism using sealed envelopes. Although the text does not add the full SNOSE details (sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed), the presence of sealed envelopes prepared by an external monitoring agency indicates the recruiters could not foresee the next assignment before enrollment, which supports low risk for allocation concealment in this report.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This is a 1-year, prospective, controlled, randomized, rater blinded, parallel-group, monocentric pilot study" and "Included patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to continue monthly intravenous NTZ 300 mg or to de-escalate to every other day subcutaneous (s.c.) INFB 250 ug." Comment: The paper explicitly states only that the study was rater-blinded; it does not state that participants or personnel delivering treatment were blinded, and the interventions were clearly different in route and schedule (IV natalizumab vs SC interferon beta-1b), making blinding of participants/personnel unlikely. Because the main outcomes included relapses, disability progression, and MRI findings, knowledge of treatment allocation could plausibly affect clinical outcomes and reporting/management, so this is high risk for performance bias overall.

Quote: "EDSS and relapses assessment was performed by an examining neurologist blinded to treatment." Quote: "MRI disease activity was assessed via subtraction MRI (sMRI) and via count of Gd+L by an expert who was blinded to clinical data." Comment: The key clinical outcomes (relapses, EDSS) were assessed by a blinded neurologist, and MRI outcomes were read by a blinded expert. These outcomes are susceptible to detection bias if unblinded, so explicit blinding of the assessors supports a low risk judgement.

Quote: "17/19 patients completed the study: one IFNB-patient withdrew consent (day 34) because she could not comply with study procedures; one NTZ-patient opted for an oral treatment (day 139). One IFNB-patient (#9) switched to rescue treatment at day 69 due to systemic side effects." Comment: Attrition was small overall (2/19 did not complete; 1 additional IFNB participant switched to rescue but follow-up completion is still described as 17/19 completed), and the reasons reported are balanced enough and not clearly indicative of differential outcome-related dropout. The trial also states that "Analysis was based on intention to treat," which further supports low risk of attrition bias in this small RCT.
